title: Confidence Intervals
text: Start loading necessary libraries data. Note structure experimental data: row corresponds single unit (participant) contains treatment assignment () observed response (). experiment, estimand interest average treatment effect (ATE), can estimated using difference means treatment control groups. can write function operate response treatment vectors use calculate observed ATE estimate.
code: library(dplyr) library(ggplot2) library(readr) anchor <- read_csv("https://stat158.berkeley.edu/spring-2026/data/anchoring/anchoring.csv") |>   mutate(d = factor(X),          y = Y) |>   select(d, y) anchor #> # A tibble: 74 × 2 #>    d         y #>    <fct> <dbl> #>  1 11     16   #>  2 11      5   #>  3 73     20   #>  4 73     40   #>  5 11     73   #>  6 73     70   #>  7 11      6.5 #>  8 73     12   #>  9 11     15   #> 10 11     70   #> # ℹ 64 more rows diff_in_means <- function(y, x) {   groups <- split(y, x)   Ybarhat0 <- mean(groups[[1]])   Ybarhat1 <- mean(groups[[2]])   Ybarhat1 - Ybarhat0 }  ate_hat <- diff_in_means(anchor$y, anchor$d) ate_hat #> [1] 15.46992

what: Impute a schedule of potential outcomes
title: Confidence Intervals
text: RBI confidence interval seeks quantify variability induced random assignment occurred outset experiment. , first need impute unobserved potential outcomes unit experiment. Let’s start creating schedule observed potential outcomes. Note schedule contains observed potential outcomes unit, unobserved potential outcomes missing. can use observed ATE estimate impute unobserved potential outcomes assuming treatment effect constant across units. likely true schedule potential outcomes, useful tool simulating random assignment process understanding variability ATE estimate.
code: schedule <- anchor |>   experiment_to_schedule(treatment = d, response = y) schedule #> # A tibble: 74 × 4 #>    d         y   Y11   Y73 #>    <fct> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> #>  1 11     16    16      NA #>  2 11      5     5      NA #>  3 73     20    NA      20 #>  4 73     40    NA      40 #>  5 11     73    73      NA #>  6 73     70    NA      70 #>  7 11      6.5   6.5    NA #>  8 73     12    NA      12 #>  9 11     15    15      NA #> 10 11     70    70      NA #> # ℹ 64 more rows imputed_schedule <- impute_unobserved(schedule, tau = ate_hat) imputed_schedule #> # A tibble: 74 × 4 #>    d         y   Y11   Y73 #>    <fct> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> #>  1 11     16   16     31.5 #>  2 11      5    5     20.5 #>  3 73     20    4.53  20   #>  4 73     40   24.5   40   #>  5 11     73   73     88.5 #>  6 73     70   54.5   70   #>  7 11      6.5  6.5   22.0 #>  8 73     12   -3.47  12   #>  9 11     15   15     30.5 #> 10 11     70   70     85.5 #> # ℹ 64 more rows

title: Confidence Intervals
text: Now schedule potential outcomes, can simulate random assignment process generate new datasets observed experimental design. Let’s start simulating single experiment. can compare original data set: units across data sets, treatment assignments observed responses may differ due random assignment process. first unit, example assigned 73 simulated experiment, observed Y1(1)Y_1(1): 31.5. original data, first unit assigned 11 observed Y1(0)Y_1(0): 16. second unit, assigned 11 original experiment simulated experiment, observed Y2(0)Y_2(0): 5 cases. can calculate ATE estimate simulated experiment. serves proof concept can simulate new experiments calculate new ATE estimates experimental design. However, single simulated experiment enough understand variability ATE estimate. need simulate many experiments get distribution ATE estimates.
code: set.seed(402) # for reproducibility sim1 <- imputed_schedule |>   sim_experiment(reps = 1) sim1 #> # A tibble: 74 × 3 #>    replicate d         y #>        <int> <fct> <dbl> #>  1         1 73    31.5  #>  2         1 11     5    #>  3         1 73    20    #>  4         1 11    24.5  #>  5         1 73    88.5  #>  6         1 73    70    #>  7         1 11     6.5  #>  8         1 11    -3.47 #>  9         1 73    30.5  #> 10         1 11    70    #> # ℹ 64 more rows anchor #> # A tibble: 74 × 2 #>    d         y #>    <fct> <dbl> #>  1 11     16   #>  2 11      5   #>  3 73     20   #>  4 73     40   #>  5 11     73   #>  6 73     70   #>  7 11      6.5 #>  8 73     12   #>  9 11     15   #> 10 11     70   #> # ℹ 64 more rows ate_hat_sim1 <- sim1 |>   summarize(ate_hat = diff_in_means(y, d)) ate_hat_sim1 #> # A tibble: 1 × 1 #>   ate_hat #>     <dbl> #> 1    17.3

title: Confidence Intervals
text: Let’s use approach simulate 5000 experiments. sim_experiment() returns 5000 simulated experiments single tall data frame, one stacked top one another, new variable called replicate indicates rows belong simulated experiment. can use variable group data calculate ATE estimate simulated experiment. can visualize distribution ATE estimates simulated experiments see centered around observed ATE estimate original experiment.
code: sim5000 <- imputed_schedule |>   sim_experiment(reps = 5000) head(sim5000) #> # A tibble: 6 × 3 #>   replicate d         y #>       <int> <fct> <dbl> #> 1         1 11    16    #> 2         1 73    20.5  #> 3         1 11     4.53 #> 4         1 73    40    #> 5         1 11    73    #> 6         1 73    70 tail(sim5000) #> # A tibble: 6 × 3 #>   replicate d         y #>       <int> <fct> <dbl> #> 1      5000 73     60   #> 2      5000 73     32   #> 3      5000 11     25   #> 4      5000 73     60   #> 5      5000 11     26.7 #> 6      5000 11     28 sim5000_estimates <- sim5000 |>   group_by(replicate) |>   summarize(ate_hat = diff_in_means(y, d)) sim5000_estimates #> # A tibble: 5,000 × 2 #>    replicate ate_hat #>        <int>   <dbl> #>  1         1    13.3 #>  2         2    21.5 #>  3         3    20.9 #>  4         4    19.8 #>  5         5    14.2 #>  6         6    16.7 #>  7         7    13.6 #>  8         8    13.9 #>  9         9    19.3 #> 10        10    16.9 #> # ℹ 4,990 more rows ggplot(sim5000_estimates, aes(x = ate_hat)) +   geom_histogram(binwidth = 0.5, color = "black") +   geom_vline(xintercept = ate_hat, color = "red", linetype = "dashed") +   labs(title = "Distribution of Estimates from Simulated Experiments",        x = "Estimated ATE",        y = "Frequency") +   theme_bw()

title: Confidence Intervals
text: 1−α1-\alpha confidence interval summarizes distribution ATE estimates simulated experiments two numbers, lower upper bounds, taken quantiles distribution.
code: alpha <- 0.05 # for a 95% confidence interval ci <- quantile(sim5000_estimates$ate_hat, probs = c(alpha/2, 1 - alpha/2)) ci #>      2.5%     97.5%  #>  6.631069 24.118810

title: Simulate an Experiment from a Schedule
text: key function simulating experiment . function takes input schedule object. objects can created using experiment_to_schedule() observed dataset treatment variable outcome variable. instead data frame contains potential outcomes unit treatment condition, can use as_schedule() convert schedule object. Let’s start example schedule: data frame contains potential outcomes 62 units Anchoring Experiment, assigned answer question referenced number 11 73 asked estimated percentage African countries United Nations. columns Y11 Y73 represent potential outcomes unit control treatment conditions, respectively. Note observed outcomes, rather potential outcomes observed treatment condition. data frame also contains factor column indicates treatment assignments. useful represents total number units assigned group, essential piece information simulating experiments mirror original design. can convert data frame schedule object using as_schedule().
code: library(readr)  anchoring_sched <- read_csv("https://stat158.berkeley.edu/spring-2026/data/anchoring/godlike_schedule_A.csv") #> Rows: 62 Columns: 3 #> ── Column specification ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Delimiter: "," #> dbl (3): d, Y11, Y73 #>  #> ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data. #> ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message. anchoring_sched$d <- factor(anchoring_sched$d) anchoring_sched #> # A tibble: 62 × 3 #>    d       Y11   Y73 #>    <fct> <dbl> <dbl> #>  1 73       15    30 #>  2 73       45    60 #>  3 73       25    40 #>  4 11       25    40 #>  5 11       20    35 #>  6 11       21    36 #>  7 73        5    20 #>  8 11        8    23 #>  9 11       20    35 #> 10 73        5    20 #> # ℹ 52 more rows schedule <- as_schedule(anchoring_sched, treatment = d, potential_outcomes = c(Y11, Y73)) schedule #> # A tibble: 62 × 3 #>    d       Y11   Y73 #>    <fct> <dbl> <dbl> #>  1 73       15    30 #>  2 73       45    60 #>  3 73       25    40 #>  4 11       25    40 #>  5 11       20    35 #>  6 11       21    36 #>  7 73        5    20 #>  8 11        8    23 #>  9 11       20    35 #> 10 73        5    20 #> # ℹ 52 more rows

what: Simulate an experiment from the schedule
title: Simulate an Experiment from a Schedule
text: Now schedule object, can simulate experiment using sim_experiment() function. function takes input schedule object number simulations run. data frame contains observed outcomes unit simulated treatment assignments. row corresponds single unit, columns represent treatment assignment observed outcome unit simulated experiment.
code: set.seed(5024) # for reproducibility simulated_experiment <- schedule |>   sim_experiment(reps = 1) simulated_experiment #> # A tibble: 62 × 3 #>    replicate d         Y #>        <int> <fct> <dbl> #>  1         1 73       30 #>  2         1 11       45 #>  3         1 73       40 #>  4         1 73       40 #>  5         1 73       35 #>  6         1 73       36 #>  7         1 11        5 #>  8         1 11        8 #>  9         1 73       35 #> 10         1 11        5 #> # ℹ 52 more rows

title: Authors and Citation
text: Andrew Bray. Author, maintainer.

title: Authors and Citation
text: Bray (2026). designrbi: Experimental Design using Randomization-Based Inference. R package version 0.0.0.9000.
code: @Manual{,   title = {designrbi: Experimental Design using Randomization-Based Inference},   author = {Andrew Bray},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 0.0.0.9000}, }

title: Experimental Design using Randomization-Based Inference
text: designrbi R package conducting statistical inference randomized experiments using randomization-based framework. offers series helpful functions. experiment_to_schedule() transform data experiment schedule potential outcomes missing outcomes showing NA. impute_unobserved() impute missing potential outcomes schedule using constant effect choice. sim_experiment() simulate one experiments based given schedule potential outcomes. as_schedule() take pre-existing data frame potential outcomes present convert schedule object.

title: Experimental Design using Randomization-Based Inference
text: Install package directly github using remotes package. See Articles link top menu guidance use package.

what: Coerce a wide data frame to a schedule of potential outcomes — as_schedule
title: Coerce a wide data frame to a schedule of potential outcomes — as_schedule
text: Attaches "schedule" class metadata attributes data frame already structured schedule potential outcomes — .e., one column per treatment level containing potential outcomes unit. convert long-format experimental data instead, see experiment_to_schedule.

title: Coerce a wide data frame to a schedule of potential outcomes — as_schedule
Coerce a wide data frame to a schedule of potential outcomes — as_schedule
text: data data frame structured schedule potential outcomes, one column per treatment level. treatment unquoted name column data contains treatment assignments, must factor. potential_outcomes tidyselect expression selecting columns contain potential outcomes, one per treatment level (e.g., c(Ycontrol, Ytreatment) starts_with("Y")).

title: Coerce a wide data frame to a schedule of potential outcomes — as_schedule
text: data frame class "schedule" metadata attributes: treatment, treatment_levels, treatment_counts, potential_outcome_cols.

title: Coerce a wide data frame to a schedule of potential outcomes — as_schedule
code: sched <- data.frame(   id = 1:6,   treatment = factor(c("control", "control", "treatment",                        "treatment", "control", "treatment")),   Ycontrol   = c(10, 12, 15, 18, 11, 17),   Ytreatment = c(11, 13, 16, 19, 10, 17) ) as_schedule(sched, treatment, c(Ycontrol, Ytreatment)) #>   id treatment Ycontrol Ytreatment #> 1  1   control       10         11 #> 2  2   control       12         13 #> 3  3 treatment       15         16 #> 4  4 treatment       18         19 #> 5  5   control       11         10 #> 6  6 treatment       17         17

what: Convert experimental data to a schedule of potential outcomes — experiment_to_schedule
title: Convert experimental data to a schedule of potential outcomes — experiment_to_schedule
text: Takes data frame long format typical experimental data — one row per unit, single response column treatment assignment column — pivots wide schedule potential outcomes attaching "schedule" class metadata. observed response unit fills assigned treatment column; potential outcome columns NA (unobserved). Use impute_unobserved fill .

title: Convert experimental data to a schedule of potential outcomes — experiment_to_schedule
Convert experimental data to a schedule of potential outcomes — experiment_to_schedule
text: data data frame one row per experimental unit. treatment unquoted name column data contains treatment assignments, must factor. response unquoted name column data contains observed response variable. outcome_prefix character string prepended treatment level name form potential outcome column names. Defaults "Y".

title: Convert experimental data to a schedule of potential outcomes — experiment_to_schedule
text: data frame class "schedule", containing original columns plus one new column per treatment level (named <outcome_prefix><level>), metadata attributes: treatment, treatment_levels, treatment_counts, potential_outcome_cols, response.

title: Convert experimental data to a schedule of potential outcomes — experiment_to_schedule
code: data <- data.frame(   id = 1:6,   treatment = factor(c("control", "control", "treatment",                        "treatment", "control", "treatment")),   response = c(10, 12, 15, 18, 11, 17) ) experiment_to_schedule(data, treatment, response) #>   id treatment response Ycontrol Ytreatment #> 1  1   control       10       10         NA #> 2  2   control       12       12         NA #> 3  3 treatment       15       NA         15 #> 4  4 treatment       18       NA         18 #> 5  5   control       11       11         NA #> 6  6 treatment       17       NA         17

title: Impute Unobserved Potential Outcomes — impute_unobserved
text: Impute unobserved potential outcomes schedule potential outcomes using specified value Individual Treatment Effect (ITE).

title: Impute Unobserved Potential Outcomes — impute_unobserved
text: schedule data frame containing schedule potential outcomes, one row per unit one column treatment level. tau numeric value Individual Treatment Effect use impute unobserved potential outcomes.

title: Impute Unobserved Potential Outcomes — impute_unobserved
text: data frame structure schedule unobserved potential outcomes imputed using specified value tau.

title: Impute Unobserved Potential Outcomes — impute_unobserved
code: data <- data.frame(  id = 1:6,  treatment = factor(c("control", "control", "treatment", "treatment", "control", "treatment")),  response = c(10, 12, 15, 18, 11, 17) ) schedule <- experiment_to_schedule(data, treatment, response)  # the fisher sharp null: tau = 0 imputed_schedule_0 <- impute_unobserved(schedule, tau = 0) print(imputed_schedule_0) #>   id treatment response Ycontrol Ytreatment #> 1  1   control       10       10         10 #> 2  2   control       12       12         12 #> 3  3 treatment       15       15         15 #> 4  4 treatment       18       18         18 #> 5  5   control       11       11         11 #> 6  6 treatment       17       17         17  # a constant shift effect size of 3 imputed_schedule_3 <- impute_unobserved(schedule, tau = 3) print(imputed_schedule_3) #>   id treatment response Ycontrol Ytreatment #> 1  1   control       10       10         13 #> 2  2   control       12       12         15 #> 3  3 treatment       15       12         15 #> 4  4 treatment       18       15         18 #> 5  5   control       11       11         14 #> 6  6 treatment       17       14         17

title: Simulate a randomized experiment — sim_experiment
text: Recreates process randomly assigning unit treatment group observing corresponding potential outcome, given complete schedule potential outcomes.

title: Simulate a randomized experiment — sim_experiment
text: schedule data frame class schedule missing potential outcomes, returned impute_unobserved(). reps positive integer giving number simulations run. Results simulation stacked row-wise identified replicate column. Defaults 1. response_name character string giving name response column output. schedules created experiment_to_schedule(), defaults original response column name. schedules created as_schedule() (wide format), original response column name, defaults "Y".

title: Simulate a randomized experiment — sim_experiment
text: data frame treatment column, response column (named according response_name), replicate integer column indicating simulation row belongs .

title: Simulate a randomized experiment — sim_experiment
code: data <- data.frame(   treatment = factor(c("control", "control", "treatment", "treatment", "control", "treatment")),   response  = c(10, 12, 15, 18, 11, 17) ) schedule <- experiment_to_schedule(data, treatment, response) imputed  <- impute_unobserved(schedule, tau = 0) sim_experiment(imputed, reps = 5) #> # A tibble: 30 × 3 #>    replicate treatment response #>        <int> <fct>        <dbl> #>  1         1 treatment       10 #>  2         1 control         12 #>  3         1 treatment       15 #>  4         1 treatment       18 #>  5         1 control         11 #>  6         1 control         17 #>  7         2 treatment       10 #>  8         2 control         12 #>  9         2 treatment       15 #> 10         2 control         18 #> # ℹ 20 more rows
